preamble: The shift from the rhotic consonant [r] to the mid-back rounded vowel [o] (or its open-mid counterpart [ɔ]) is a form of r-vocalization and compensatory lengthening. It occurs in non-rhotic dialects where the consonant is lost as a distinct segment, leaving behind a lengthened or modified back vowel.

examples.note: Historical loss of the rhotic resulted in the stabilization of the long back vowel.
Coda r vocalizes into a central vowel quality that often rounds toward [o] in certain environments.
